Present Estates — Fee Simple Absolute.rule: A fee simple absolute is an estate of indefinite duration with no conditions or limitations.
Present Estates — Fee Simple Absolute.application: Because the grant contains no limitations, a fee simple absolute is created.

Present Estates — Fee Simple Determinabl.rule: A fee simple determinable automatically terminates upon the occurrence of a stated event.
Present Estates — Fee Simple Determinabl.application: Because the estate ends automatically on the event, it is a fee simple determinable.

Future Interest — Possibility of Reverte.rule: A possibility of reverter is a future interest retained by the grantor following a fee simple determinable.
Future Interest — Possibility of Reverte.application: Because the grantor retains the future interest, a possibility of reverter exists.

Present Estates — Fee Simple Subject to.rule: A fee simple subject to condition subsequent does not automatically terminate but may be terminated by the grantor upon breach of a condition.
buzzwords: fee simple subject to condition subsequent
Present Estates — Fee Simple Subject to.application: Because termination requires action by the grantor, the estate is subject to condition subsequent.

Future Interest — Right of Entry.rule: A right of entry is a future interest retained by the grantor following a fee simple subject to condition subsequent.
Future Interest — Right of Entry.application: Because the grantor must act to terminate, a right of entry exists.

Present Estates — Fee Simple Subject to.rule: A fee simple subject to executory limitation automatically transfers to a third party upon the occurrence of a stated event.
buzzwords: fee simple subject to executory limitation
Present Estates — Fee Simple Subject to.application: Because the estate shifts to a third party, an executory limitation exists.

Future Interest — Executory Interest.rule: An executory interest is a future interest held by a third party that divests a prior estate upon the occurrence of a condition.
Future Interest — Executory Interest.application: Because the future interest divests another estate, it is an executory interest.

Life Estate.rule: A life estate is an estate measured by the life of a specified person.
Life Estate.application: Because the duration is tied to a life, a life estate is created.

Future Interest — Remainder.rule: A remainder is a future interest that becomes possessory upon the natural termination of a prior estate.
Future Interest — Remainder.application: Because the interest follows a life estate, it is a remainder.

Vested Remainder.rule: A vested remainder is a remainder held by an ascertained person with no condition precedent.
Vested Remainder.application: Because the taker is known and unconditional, the remainder is vested.

Contingent Remainder.rule: A contingent remainder is a remainder that depends on an unfulfilled condition precedent or an unascertained person.
Contingent Remainder.application: Because the taker is not yet known, the remainder is contingent.

Doctrine of Waste.rule: A life tenant has a duty not to commit waste that unreasonably impairs the future interest.
Doctrine of Waste.application: Because the tenant damaged the property, waste occurred.

Rule Against Perpetuities — General Rule.rule: A future interest is invalid unless it must vest, if at all, within 21 years after a life in being at the creation of the interest.
Rule Against Perpetuities — General Rule.application: Because the interest may vest too late, it is void.

Tenancy in Common.rule: A tenancy in common allows each co-tenant to hold an undivided interest with no right of survivorship.
Tenancy in Common.application: Because there is no survivorship right, the estate is a tenancy in common.

Joint Tenancy.rule: A joint tenancy requires the four unities of time, title, interest, and possession and includes a right of survivorship.
Joint Tenancy.application: Because all four unities exist, a joint tenancy is created.

Creation of Joint Tenancy.rule: A joint tenancy must be expressly created with clear survivorship language.
Creation of Joint Tenancy.application: Because survivorship language was used, joint tenancy exists.

Severance of Joint Tenancy.rule: A joint tenancy is severed when one co-tenant transfers their interest, destroying the unities.
Severance of Joint Tenancy.application: Because a co-tenant conveyed their interest, severance occurred.

Tenancy by the Entirety.rule: Tenancy by the entirety is a concurrent estate between married spouses with a right of survivorship and protection from unilateral transfer.
Tenancy by the Entirety.application: Because the owners are married, tenancy by the entirety applies.

Rights of Co-Tenants — Possession.rule: Each co-tenant has an equal right to possess the whole property.
Rights of Co-Tenants — Possession.application: Because each co-tenant may occupy the property, possession is shared.

Ouster.rule: Ouster occurs when one co-tenant wrongfully excludes another from possession.
Ouster.application: Because access was denied, ouster occurred.

Co-Tenant Rent Liability.rule: A co-tenant in possession is not liable for rent absent ouster.
Co-Tenant Rent Liability.application: Because no ouster occurred, rent is not owed.

Co-Tenant Expenses — Contribution.rule: Co-tenants must contribute to necessary expenses such as taxes and mortgage payments.
Co-Tenant Expenses — Contribution.application: Because expenses were necessary, contribution is required.

Co-Tenant Repairs.rule: A co-tenant is not entitled to contribution for repairs unless they are necessary.
Co-Tenant Repairs.application: Because the repair was not necessary, no contribution is owed.

Co-Tenant Improvements.rule: A co-tenant is not entitled to contribution for improvements but may recover their value upon partition.
Co-Tenant Improvements.application: Because the improvement increased value, recovery may occur at partition.

Partition — General Rule.rule: Any co-tenant may seek partition of the property.
Partition — General Rule.application: Because a co-tenant requested partition, the action is permitted.

Partition in Kind.rule: Partition in kind physically divides the property and is preferred when feasible.
Partition in Kind.application: Because the land can be divided, partition in kind applies.

Partition by Sale.rule: Partition by sale is ordered when physical division is impracticable or inequitable.
Partition by Sale.application: Because division is impracticable, partition by sale is ordered.

Leasehold Estates — General Rule.rule: A leasehold estate grants the tenant the right to exclusive possession of property for a period of time.
Leasehold Estates — General Rule.application: Because the tenant has exclusive possession, a leasehold estate exists.

Tenancy for Years.rule: A tenancy for years lasts for a fixed, definite period of time and terminates automatically.
Tenancy for Years.application: Because the lease has a definite term, it is a tenancy for years.

Periodic Tenancy.rule: A periodic tenancy continues for successive periods until terminated by proper notice.
Periodic Tenancy.application: Because the lease renews monthly, it is a periodic tenancy.

Tenancy at Will.rule: A tenancy at will continues until terminated by either party without advance notice.
Tenancy at Will.application: Because either party may terminate at any time, a tenancy at will exists.

Tenancy at Sufferance.rule: A tenancy at sufferance arises when a tenant wrongfully remains in possession after lease termination.
Tenancy at Sufferance.application: Because the tenant stayed after expiration, a tenancy at sufferance exists.

Tenant Duties — Rent.rule: A tenant has a duty to pay rent as specified in the lease.
Tenant Duties — Rent.application: Because rent was not paid, the tenant breached the lease.

Landlord Duties — Deliver Possession.rule: A landlord must deliver actual possession of the premises at the start of the lease.
Landlord Duties — Deliver Possession.application: Because the premises were occupied, the landlord breached the duty.

Implied Covenant of Quiet Enjoyment.rule: Every lease includes an implied covenant that the tenant will have quiet enjoyment without interference by the landlord.
Implied Covenant of Quiet Enjoyment.application: Because the landlord interfered, the covenant was breached.

Constructive Eviction.rule: Constructive eviction occurs when the landlord substantially interferes with use and the tenant vacates within a reasonable time.
Constructive Eviction.application: Because the tenant vacated due to interference, constructive eviction occurred.

Actual Eviction.rule: Actual eviction occurs when the tenant is physically excluded from the premises.
Actual Eviction.application: Because the tenant was locked out, actual eviction occurred.

Implied Warranty of Habitability.rule: Residential leases include an implied warranty that premises are fit for human habitation.
Implied Warranty of Habitability.application: Because essential services were lacking, the warranty was breached.

Tenant Remedies — Habitability.rule: If the implied warranty of habitability is breached, the tenant may withhold rent, repair and deduct, or terminate the lease.
Tenant Remedies — Habitability.application: Because the premises were uninhabitable, the tenant could withhold rent.

Landlord Remedies — Tenant Breach.rule: If a tenant breaches, the landlord may evict, sue for rent, or mitigate damages.
Landlord Remedies — Tenant Breach.application: Because the tenant abandoned, the landlord must mitigate damages.

Duty to Mitigate.rule: A landlord has a duty to mitigate damages by making reasonable efforts to re-rent the premises.
Duty to Mitigate.application: Because the landlord attempted to re-rent, the duty was satisfied.

Assignment.rule: An assignment transfers the tenant’s entire remaining interest in the lease.
Assignment.application: Because the entire term was transferred, an assignment occurred.

Sublease.rule: A sublease transfers less than the tenant’s entire remaining interest.
Sublease.application: Because part of the term was retained, a sublease occurred.

Privity of Estate.rule: Privity of estate exists between parties who hold successive interests in the same property.
Privity of Estate.application: Because interests were successive, privity of estate existed.

Privity of Contract.rule: Privity of contract exists between parties to the lease agreement.
Privity of Contract.application: Because the parties signed the lease, privity of contract exists.

Real Estate Contracts — General Rule.rule: A contract for the sale of land requires mutual assent, consideration, and compliance with the statute of frauds.
buzzwords: contract for the sale of land
Real Estate Contracts — General Rule.application: Because the parties agreed and consideration was exchanged, a valid contract exists.

Statute of Frauds — Land Contracts.rule: A contract for the sale of land must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged.
Statute of Frauds — Land Contracts.application: Because the contract was signed in writing, the statute of frauds is satisfied.

Statute of Frauds — Essential Terms.rule: The writing must identify the parties, describe the land, and state the price.
Statute of Frauds — Essential Terms.application: Because the writing included the land and price, it is sufficient.

Part Performance Exception.rule: Part performance removes a land contract from the statute of frauds when the buyer takes possession, pays consideration, or makes improvements.
Part Performance Exception.application: Because the buyer took possession and made improvements, the contract is enforceable.

Equitable Conversion.rule: Under equitable conversion, the buyer is treated as the equitable owner of the land once the contract is signed.
Equitable Conversion.application: Because the contract was executed, equitable conversion applies.

Risk of Loss.rule: After equitable conversion, the risk of loss passes to the buyer unless the contract provides otherwise.
Risk of Loss.application: Because the buyer bore the risk, loss is allocated to the buyer.

Marketable Title — General Rule.rule: At closing, the seller must deliver marketable title free from unreasonable doubt.
Marketable Title — General Rule.application: Because title was free from defects, it was marketable.

Unmarketable Title — Defects.rule: Title is unmarketable if it is subject to encumbrances, adverse possession claims, or zoning violations.
Unmarketable Title — Defects.application: Because an encumbrance existed, title was unmarketable.

Time Is of the Essence.rule: Time is not of the essence in land contracts unless expressly stated.
Time Is of the Essence.application: Because time was not stated as essential, delay did not breach the contract.

Seller Remedies — Buyer Breach.rule: If the buyer breaches, the seller may retain the deposit, sue for damages, or seek specific performance.
Seller Remedies — Buyer Breach.application: Because the buyer failed to close, the seller may seek specific performance.

Buyer Remedies — Seller Breach.rule: If the seller breaches, the buyer may recover damages or seek specific performance.
Buyer Remedies — Seller Breach.application: Because the seller failed to convey, the buyer may compel performance.

Merger Doctrine.rule: At closing, the contract merges into the deed and contractual obligations are extinguished.
Merger Doctrine.application: Because closing occurred, prior contract terms merged into the deed.

Implied Covenants — Deed Delivery.rule: Delivery of a deed requires intent to transfer and acceptance by the grantee.
Implied Covenants — Deed Delivery.application: Because the grantor intended transfer and the grantee accepted, delivery occurred.

Deeds — General Rule.rule: A deed transfers an interest in land and requires execution and delivery to be effective.
Deeds — General Rule.application: Because the deed was executed and delivered, title transferred.

Execution of Deed.rule: Execution of a deed requires the grantor’s signature and compliance with any required formalities.
Execution of Deed.application: Because the grantor signed the deed, execution is satisfied.

Delivery of Deed.rule: Delivery of a deed requires intent to make the deed presently effective.
Delivery of Deed.application: Because the grantor intended immediate transfer, delivery occurred.

Acceptance of Deed.rule: Acceptance of a deed by the grantee is required and is presumed if the transfer benefits the grantee.
Acceptance of Deed.application: Because the conveyance benefited the grantee, acceptance is presumed.

Quitclaim Deed.rule: A quitclaim deed conveys whatever interest the grantor has without warranties of title.
Quitclaim Deed.application: Because no warranties were given, the deed is a quitclaim deed.

General Warranty Deed.rule: A general warranty deed provides warranties against defects in title arising before and during the grantor’s ownership.
General Warranty Deed.application: Because the deed warranted title against all defects, it is a general warranty deed.

Special Warranty Deed.rule: A special warranty deed provides warranties only against defects arising during the grantor’s ownership.
Special Warranty Deed.application: Because the warranty was limited to the grantor’s period, it is a special warranty deed.

Covenant of Seisin.rule: The covenant of seisin warrants that the grantor owns the estate conveyed.
Covenant of Seisin.application: Because the grantor owned the land, the covenant was not breached.

Covenant of Right to Convey.rule: The covenant of right to convey warrants that the grantor has the authority to transfer the property.
Covenant of Right to Convey.application: Because the grantor had authority, the covenant was satisfied.

Covenant Against Encumbrances.rule: The covenant against encumbrances warrants that the land is free from undisclosed encumbrances.
Covenant Against Encumbrances.application: Because an easement existed, the covenant was breached.

Covenant of Quiet Enjoyment.rule: The covenant of quiet enjoyment warrants that the grantee will not be disturbed by superior title.
Covenant of Quiet Enjoyment.application: Because the grantee was evicted by a titleholder, the covenant was breached.

Covenant of Warranty.rule: The covenant of warranty obligates the grantor to defend the grantee against lawful claims of title.
Covenant of Warranty.application: Because a third party asserted title, the grantor must defend.

Covenant of Further Assurances.rule: The covenant of further assurances requires the grantor to take reasonable steps to perfect title.
Covenant of Further Assurances.application: Because corrective action was needed, the grantor must comply.

Recording Acts — Purpose.rule: Recording acts protect bona fide purchasers from prior unrecorded interests.
Recording Acts — Purpose.application: Because the buyer recorded first, protection applies.

Notice Recording Statute.rule: Under a notice statute, a bona fide purchaser without notice of a prior interest prevails.
Notice Recording Statute.application: Because the buyer lacked notice, the buyer prevails.

Race Recording Statute.rule: Under a race statute, the first party to record prevails regardless of notice.
Race Recording Statute.application: Because the buyer recorded first, priority applies.

Race-Notice Recording Statute.rule: Under a race-notice statute, a bona fide purchaser who records first prevails.
Race-Notice Recording Statute.application: Because the buyer recorded first without notice, the buyer prevails.

Types of Notice — Actual Notice.rule: Actual notice exists when a purchaser has direct knowledge of a prior interest.
Types of Notice — Actual Notice.application: Because the purchaser knew of the deed, actual notice existed.

Types of Notice — Record Notice.rule: Record notice exists when a prior interest is properly recorded.
Types of Notice — Record Notice.application: Because the deed was recorded, record notice applied.

Types of Notice — Inquiry Notice.rule: Inquiry notice exists when circumstances would prompt a reasonable purchaser to investigate.
Types of Notice — Inquiry Notice.application: Because possession suggested another interest, inquiry notice applied.

Shelter Rule.rule: Under the shelter rule, a transferee takes the same rights as the transferor.
Shelter Rule.application: Because the transferor was protected, the transferee is protected.

Mortgage — General Rule.rule: A mortgage is a security interest in land intended to secure repayment of a debt.
Mortgage — General Rule.application: Because the loan is secured by land, a mortgage exists.

Mortgage — Creation.rule: A mortgage requires a debt and an intent to secure the debt with land.
Mortgage — Creation.application: Because the parties intended to secure the loan with land, the mortgage is valid.

Mortgage — Lien Theory.rule: Under lien theory, the mortgagee holds a lien while the mortgagor retains title.
Mortgage — Lien Theory.application: Because the lender holds only a lien, lien theory applies.

Mortgage — Title Theory.rule: Under title theory, the mortgagee holds legal title until the mortgage is satisfied.
Mortgage — Title Theory.application: Because title passed to the lender, title theory applies.

Mortgage — Priority Rule.rule: Mortgage priority is determined by the order of recording unless modified by statute.
Mortgage — Priority Rule.application: Because the mortgage was recorded first, it has priority.

Purchase Money Mortgage — General Rule.rule: A purchase money mortgage has priority over prior liens against the mortgagor.
Purchase Money Mortgage — General Rule.application: Because the loan enabled purchase, the mortgage has priority.

Purchase Money Mortgage — Vendor vs Lend.rule: A vendor purchase money mortgage has priority over a third-party lender purchase money mortgage.
Purchase Money Mortgage — Vendor vs Lend.application: Because the seller financed the purchase, the vendor mortgage prevails.

Future Advance Mortgage.rule: A mortgage securing future advances has priority from the date of recording.
Future Advance Mortgage.application: Because the mortgage covered future advances, priority relates back.

Transfer of Mortgaged Property.rule: A mortgagor may transfer property subject to the mortgage, but the mortgage remains attached.
Transfer of Mortgaged Property.application: Because the buyer took subject to the mortgage, the lien remains.

Assumption of Mortgage.rule: When a buyer assumes the mortgage, the buyer becomes personally liable for the debt.
Assumption of Mortgage.application: Because the buyer assumed the mortgage, personal liability exists.

Foreclosure — General Rule.rule: Foreclosure terminates the mortgagor’s equity of redemption and junior interests.
Foreclosure — General Rule.application: Because foreclosure occurred, junior liens were extinguished.

Necessary Parties — Foreclosure.rule: Junior lienholders must be joined in a foreclosure action to terminate their interests.
Necessary Parties — Foreclosure.application: Because the junior lienholder was joined, the lien was terminated.

Effect of Foreclosure on Senior Interest.rule: Senior interests are not affected by foreclosure and remain attached to the property.
Effect of Foreclosure on Senior Interest.application: Because the lien was senior, it survived foreclosure.

Equity of Redemption.rule: The mortgagor has the right to redeem the property by paying the debt before foreclosure.
Equity of Redemption.application: Because the mortgagor paid before foreclosure, redemption occurred.

Statutory Right of Redemption.rule: Some states allow redemption after foreclosure by statute.
Statutory Right of Redemption.application: Because the statute allows post-sale redemption, the right applies.

Deficiency Judgment.rule: If foreclosure sale proceeds are insufficient, the lender may seek a deficiency judgment.
Deficiency Judgment.application: Because the sale did not satisfy the debt, a deficiency judgment may be sought.

Anti-Deficiency Statutes.rule: Anti-deficiency statutes may bar lenders from obtaining deficiency judgments.
Anti-Deficiency Statutes.application: Because the statute applies, no deficiency judgment is allowed.

Easements — General Rule.rule: An easement is a nonpossessory interest that grants the right to use another’s land.
Easements — General Rule.application: Because the interest grants use without possession, it is an easement.

Easement Appurtenant.rule: An easement appurtenant benefits a dominant estate and burdens a servient estate.
Easement Appurtenant.application: Because the easement benefits the land, it is appurtenant.

Easement in Gross.rule: An easement in gross benefits a person rather than a parcel of land.
Easement in Gross.application: Because the easement benefits an individual, it is in gross.

Creation of Easement — Express Grant.rule: An easement may be created by express grant that satisfies the statute of frauds.
Creation of Easement — Express Grant.application: Because the easement was created in writing, it is valid.

Creation of Easement — Implication.rule: An easement may be implied when prior use was apparent, continuous, and reasonably necessary.
Creation of Easement — Implication.application: Because the prior use was continuous and necessary, an implied easement exists.

Creation of Easement — Necessity.rule: An easement by necessity arises when land is landlocked and access is necessary.
Creation of Easement — Necessity.application: Because the land lacks access, an easement by necessity exists.

Creation of Easement — Prescription.rule: An easement by prescription arises from open, notorious, adverse, and continuous use.
Creation of Easement — Prescription.application: Because use was open and adverse, a prescriptive easement arose.

Scope of Easement.rule: The scope of an easement is limited to the terms of its creation.
Scope of Easement.application: Because the use exceeded the grant, the scope was violated.

Termination of Easement.rule: An easement may be terminated by merger, release, abandonment, prescription, or necessity ending.
Termination of Easement.application: Because the dominant and servient estates merged, the easement terminated.

Real Covenants — General Rule.rule: A real covenant is a promise concerning land that runs with the land at law.
Real Covenants — General Rule.application: Because the promise binds successors, it is a real covenant.

Real Covenant — Elements.rule: A real covenant requires writing, intent, touch and concern, and privity.
Real Covenant — Elements.application: Because all elements are met, the covenant runs with the land.

Horizontal Privity.rule: Horizontal privity requires the covenant to be made in connection with a transfer of land.
Horizontal Privity.application: Because the covenant was made during conveyance, horizontal privity exists.

Vertical Privity.rule: Vertical privity requires a successor to hold the same estate as the original party.
Vertical Privity.application: Because the successor holds the same estate, vertical privity exists.

Equitable Servitudes — General Rule.rule: An equitable servitude is a covenant enforceable in equity that binds successors with notice.
Equitable Servitudes — General Rule.application: Because the buyer had notice, the servitude is enforceable.

Equitable Servitude — Elements.rule: An equitable servitude requires intent, touch and concern, and notice.
Equitable Servitude — Elements.application: Because the purchaser had notice and intent existed, the servitude applies.

Notice — Servitudes.rule: Notice for servitudes may be actual, record, or inquiry notice.
Notice — Servitudes.application: Because possession was visible, inquiry notice existed.

Common Scheme Doctrine.rule: Under a common scheme, restrictions apply to all lots when a developer intends uniform restrictions.
Common Scheme Doctrine.application: Because a common plan existed, the restriction applies.

Termination of Covenants and Servitudes.rule: Covenants and servitudes may terminate by release, merger, changed conditions, or abandonment.
Termination of Covenants and Servitudes.application: Because conditions changed drastically, the servitude terminated.

Zoning — General Rule.rule: Zoning ordinances regulate land use pursuant to the state’s police power.
Zoning — General Rule.application: Because the ordinance regulates land use, zoning authority applies.

Zoning — Constitutionality.rule: Zoning ordinances are constitutional if they are rationally related to a legitimate government purpose.
Zoning — Constitutionality.application: Because the ordinance promotes public welfare, it is constitutional.

Zoning — Variance.rule: A variance permits deviation from a zoning ordinance when strict enforcement would cause undue hardship.
Zoning — Variance.application: Because strict enforcement causes hardship, a variance may be granted.

Zoning — Use Variance vs Area Variance.rule: A use variance permits a prohibited land use, while an area variance allows deviation from dimensional requirements.
Zoning — Use Variance vs Area Variance.application: Because the variance affects lot size, it is an area variance.

Nonconforming Use.rule: A nonconforming use is an existing use that does not comply with new zoning regulations.
Nonconforming Use.application: Because the use existed before rezoning, it is nonconforming.

Nonconforming Use — Limitation.rule: A nonconforming use may continue but may not be expanded or resumed after abandonment.
Nonconforming Use — Limitation.application: Because the owner attempted expansion, the use is prohibited.

Eminent Domain — General Rule.rule: Eminent domain allows the government to take private property for public use with just compensation.
Eminent Domain — General Rule.application: Because the taking is for public use, compensation is required.

Public Use Requirement.rule: Public use includes public purpose such as economic development.
Public Use Requirement.application: Because the taking serves economic development, public use exists.

Just Compensation.rule: Just compensation is measured by the fair market value of the property at the time of the taking.
Just Compensation.application: Because compensation equals market value, the requirement is satisfied.

Regulatory Taking.rule: A regulatory taking occurs when a regulation deprives property of all economically viable use.
Regulatory Taking.application: Because the regulation eliminated all value, a taking occurred.

Temporary Taking.rule: A temporary regulation that denies use of property may constitute a compensable taking.
Temporary Taking.application: Because use was denied temporarily, compensation may be required.

Exactions.rule: Land-use exactions must have an essential nexus and rough proportionality to the government interest.
Exactions.application: Because the exaction lacks proportionality, it is invalid.

Adverse Possession — General Rule.rule: Adverse possession allows a possessor to acquire title by open, notorious, actual, exclusive, adverse, and continuous possession for the statutory period.
Adverse Possession — General Rule.application: Because possession satisfied all elements for the statutory period, title was acquired.

Actual Possession.rule: Actual possession requires physical occupation of the land in a manner appropriate to its nature.
Actual Possession.application: Because the possessor physically occupied the land, actual possession existed.

Open and Notorious.rule: Possession is open and notorious when it is visible and obvious such that the true owner is on notice.
Open and Notorious.application: Because the use was visible, the owner was on notice.

Exclusive Possession.rule: Exclusive possession requires that the possessor not share control of the property with the true owner or the public.
Exclusive Possession.application: Because the possessor excluded others, possession was exclusive.

Adverse and Hostile.rule: Adverse possession requires possession without permission of the true owner.
Adverse and Hostile.application: Because the owner did not consent, possession was hostile.

Continuous Possession.rule: Continuous possession requires uninterrupted possession for the statutory period.
Continuous Possession.application: Because possession was uninterrupted, continuity was satisfied.

Statutory Period.rule: The statutory period for adverse possession is set by state law.
Statutory Period.application: Because the statutory period elapsed, adverse possession is complete.

Tacking.rule: Successive possessors may tack periods of possession if there is privity between them.
Tacking.application: Because privity existed, possession periods were tacked.

Privity — Tacking.rule: Privity exists when possession is transferred voluntarily between possessors.
Privity — Tacking.application: Because the transfer was voluntary, privity existed.

Disabilities — General Rule.rule: The statutory period is tolled if the true owner is under a disability at the start of adverse possession.
Disabilities — General Rule.application: Because the owner was disabled at entry, the statute was tolled.

Types of Disabilities.rule: Recognized disabilities include minority, mental incapacity, and imprisonment.
Types of Disabilities.application: Because the owner was a minor, the disability applied.

Color of Title.rule: Color of title exists when a possessor enters under a defective written instrument.
Color of Title.application: Because entry was under a defective deed, color of title existed.

Constructive Adverse Possession.rule: Under color of title, constructive adverse possession extends to the entire parcel described in the instrument.
Constructive Adverse Possession.application: Because possession was under color of title, it extended to the whole parcel.

Boundary Disputes — Acquiescence.rule: Boundary by acquiescence may be established when neighbors accept a boundary for a long period.
Boundary Disputes — Acquiescence.application: Because both parties accepted the boundary, acquiescence applied.
